title: Coelum Terrae and Aula Lucis — Thomas Vaughan (Eugenius Philalethes)
subtitle: The Magician's Heavenly Chaos and the Light of the Court — Vaughan's Hermetic cosmology of the First Matter, the Virgin Mercury, and the two worlds

summary.one_line: Vaughan's twin works expounding the First Matter as Coelum Terrae, the celestial earth, and the Hermetic vision of light as the bridge between visible and invisible worlds.
summary.scope: Covers the First Matter as universal sperm, the Kabbalistic doctrine of two sanctuaries (above/below), the Virgin Mercury as Coelum Terrae, the tears of Hyanthe allegory, and the Aula Lucis doctrine of spiritual light.

sources.label: Thomas Vaughan — Coelum Terrae (Magia Adamica, 1650, via A.E. Waite edition)
Jean Albert Belin — The Adventures of an Unknown Philosopher / Aula Lucis (Les avantures du philosophe inconnu, 1646; English tr. in MS Sloane 3641)

body.text: Said the Kabalist: 'The building of the Sanctuary which is here below is framed according to that of the Sanctuary which is above.' Here we have two worlds, visible and invisible, and two universal Natures, visible and invisible, out of which both those worlds proceeded. The passive universal Nature was made in the image of the active universal one, and the conformity of both worlds or Sanctuaries consists in the original conformity of their principles. (Coelum Terrae)

Vaughan's two-sanctuary doctrine recapitulates the Platonic chorismos (the cleft between worlds) but resolves it through the Coelum Terrae — a 'middle nature' that is neither purely spiritual nor purely material. This corresponds exactly to Steiner's concept of the astral plane as a 'soul-world' mediating between the physical and the spiritual. In Layayoga, this middle nature is the sukshma sharira (subtle body) through which prana flows, and the 'universal agent' and 'universal patient' map to Shiva and Shakti. The Tantraloka describes this as the spanda (vibration) that bridges pure consciousness and manifest form. Vaughan's 'invisible nature' is the daimonic intermediator — compare to Proclus' threefold order where daimons provide 'an escort for creation.'

This fine substance is the child of the elements and it is a most pure sweet virgin, for nothing as yet hath been generated out of her. But if at any time she breeds it is by the fire of Nature, for that is her husband. She is no animal, no vegetable, no mineral, neither is she extracted out of animals, vegetables or minerals, but she is pre-existent to them all, for she is the mother of them. She yields to nothing but love, for her end is generation and that was never yet performed by violence. (Coelum Terrae)

The 'virgin Mercury' that yields only 'to love, not violence' is the alchemical image of kundalini shakti in her unaroused state — coiled, virginal, awaiting the touch of the inner fire (the 'fire of Nature' as husband). In Layayoga, this is the state of prana before it is awakened through kewala kumbhaka: the breath is a 'virgin' until the daimonic fire of aspiration stirs it. Vaughan's insistence that she is 'pre-existent to all' marks her as the avyakta (undifferentiated) of Samkhya. In PGM terms, she is the daimonic materia that must be 'skinned' and 'purified' in the Helios egg ritual before she can be impregnated by the solar Nous. Steiner's description of the 'lotus flowers' (chakras) as virginal spiritual organs awaiting ensoulment by the Ich is the exact parallel.

I could perceive a silent dew / Steal down her cheeks, lest it should stain / Those cheeks where only smiles should reign. / The tears stream'd down for haste and all / In chains of liquid pearl did fall. / ... It holds my heart, though now 'tis spill'd / And into waters all distill'd. (from the Hyanthe poem, Coelum Terrae) ... Arnoldus de Villa Nova: 'It is a stone and no stone, spirit, soul, and body; which if thou dissolvest, it will be dissolved; and if thou dost coagulate it, it will be coagulated; and if thou dost make it fly, it will fly; for it is volatile or flying and clear as a tear.'

Hyanthe's tears — the 'chains of liquid pearl' — are not sentiment but the alchemical dissolvent itself: the permanent water (aqua permanens) that dissolves and regenerates. This is the 'water which wets not the hands' of the Belin text. In Layayoga, these tears correspond to the amrita (nectar) that drips from the sahasrara chakra when kundalini reaches the crown — the 'rain of ambrosia' that dissolves the knots of the subtle body. Steiner's 'Christ as the moisture of the world' finds its echo here: the tears are the spiritual fluid of compassion that dissolves the rigidity of the lower self. The daimon path reads this as the systasis 'skinning' — the tears of Hyanthe are the first flowing of the daimonic presence that precedes the full union.

Belin: 'The Philosophical Stone is a substance of the mineral kind, the most perfect that can be, having in itself a most perfect mixture of the elements... God has been willing to imprint his image upon Nature; as he is one, and from this unity three persons are derived, so he willed that matter should be but one, and from this unity there should go forth three kingdoms; the mineral, the vegetable and the animal.' (The Adventures of an Unknown Philosopher)

Belin's Trinitarian matter — one substance flowing into three kingdoms — mirrors the Tantraloka's doctrine of ekam eva advitiyam (one without a second) manifesting as the three gunas: sattva, rajas, tamas. The mineral-vegetable-animal triad corresponds to the three levels of tattva evolution in Samkhya. Steiner's threefold social order (spiritual-legal-economic) is a sociological recapitulation of this same ontological Trinity. In daimon work, the 'one matter' is the daimonic substance itself — that single essence which, when contacted, irradiates all three kingdoms of the practitioner's life: body (mineral), vitality (vegetable), and consciousness (animal). The Belin text's trinitarian formulation is thus a Hermetic gloss on the systasis: one daimon, threefold manifestation.

issues.title: Aula Lucis text not yet directly quoted
issues.description: The belin.html file contains 'The Adventures of an Unknown Philosopher' by Belin, not explicitly Vaughan's Aula Lucis. A separate aula_lucis.html exists in the source directory and should be consulted for the full Aula Lucis material.

issues.title: Vaughan's Rosicrucian brother passage not yet extracted
issues.summary: The five gates passage from the Rosicrucian brother (Torture the Eagle till she weeps) contains important process symbolism not yet incorporated as a passage.

versions.summary: Initial RO with 8 passages (4 source, 4 commentary) drawn from Vaughan's Coelum Terrae and Belin's Adventures of an Unknown Philosopher. Covers two-world cosmology, Virgin Mercury, Hyanthe tears, and Trinitarian matter.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state with 8 passages from Coelum Terrae and Belin
